query: Vue slots scoped slots interview question

competitors.label: Slots
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue page for default, named, and scoped slots.
theirStrengths: The official slots guide is authoritative on slot syntax, named regions, fallback content, and scoped slots.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly framed around why scoped slots are a safe child-to-parent data exposure pattern and where they show up in headless component design.

realWorldUseCases: Common real-world use cases - Table components (you provide column templates) - List / virtual scroller components - Data fetcher components - Form builders - Headless UI components
followUpConfusion: This is inversion of control, not two-way binding.

realWorldUseCases: The official guide covers slot mechanics comprehensively, but it is less compact on headless-component and list-renderer use cases.
actionableExamples: It includes default, named, and scoped slot examples, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: The docs explain scoped slots well, but our page is more direct about why slot props do not violate one-way data flow.

actionableExamples: Keep one compact renderless-component example so the slots page does not stay only at the wrapper-component level.

competitors.winReason: The official slots guide is canonical, but our page is stronger on why scoped slots matter in reusable component architecture and on the one-way-flow follow-up.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because slots, component composition, and data flow are core Vue interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong broad Vue interview-prep resource with component-pattern coverage.
theirGaps: The landing page is not dedicated to slot types or slot-prop semantics.

realWorldUseCases: Scoped slot (slot props) Child exposes data to the parent’s slot template Reusable logic components (List, Table, Fetcher, Virtualizer)
followUpConfusion: The child is not mutating parent state . It is only exposing data .

realWorldUseCases: The bank covers Vue composition patterns broadly, but it is less focused on this exact default-versus-named-versus-scoped slots decision.
actionableExamples: Because GreatFrontEnd emphasizes solutions and examples, the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about why slot props do not break one-way data flow.

actionableExamples: Keep one tiny slot-props example with a list item or table row so the example remains concrete.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a broad interview resource, but our page is more explicit about slot types, slot-prop semantics, and why this is inversion of control rather than two-way binding.

competitors.label: The Complete Guide to Vue.js Slots
competitors.selectionReason: Vue School has a strong direct tutorial on default, named, and scoped slots.
theirStrengths: It is a direct slots tutorial and therefore a strong comparator for syntax and examples.
theirGaps: It is less interview-shaped on the child-to-parent data-flow clarification and on reusable headless-component framing.

realWorldUseCases: Child controls logic, parent controls rendering
followUpConfusion: This is inversion of control, not two-way binding.

realWorldUseCases: The tutorial already explains slot types and rendering flexibility, so use-case coverage is close.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong syntax tutorial, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about why scoped slots expose data safely without violating one-way flow.

actionableExamples: Keep the fetcher or virtualizer examples short enough that the page stays interview-sized.

competitors.winReason: Vue School is a strong slots tutorial, but our page is sharper on the architectural takeaway: scoped slots are inversion of control, not a data-flow violation.
